schools.summary: Gill: this was when Moses and the Israelites were at Succoth, shortly after their coming out of Egypt; Matthew Henry: care is here taken to perpetuate the remembrance of the preservation of Israel's firstborn; the witnesses note the LORD speaking to Moses to give the law of the firstborn, just after the great deliverance — the opening of the command that consecrates the firstborn to God (God speaking to fix the memorial of the night of the Passover: 'the LORD spake unto Moses, saying'; the time and place, that this came at Succoth, shortly after their coming out of Egypt, while the deliverance was fresh; the occasion, that God now gives the law of the firstborn to perpetuate the remembrance of how he had preserved Israel's firstborn when he smote Egypt's; that what follows is no small matter but the consecrating of the firstborn to God, a standing memorial of the redemption; that God, having delivered his people, at once gives them ordinances to keep the memory of it alive in every generation).

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses
saying
God giving the law of the firstborn just after the deliverance
perpetuating the remembrance of how God preserved Israel's firstborn when he smote Egypt's

schools.summary: Gill: 'sanctify unto me all the firstborn,' that is, of males, as the Targum adds, for those and not females were sacrificed or redeemed; this sanctification was a setting them apart for sacred use; the witnesses note God commanding that all the firstborn, of man and beast, that opened the womb be sanctified to him, for they were his — the consecrating of the firstborn as God's peculiar portion, a memorial of the sparing in Egypt (God laying his claim on the firstborn: 'Sanctify unto me all the firstborn, whatsoever openeth the womb... both of man and of beast: it is mine'; the command, 'Sanctify unto me all the firstborn,' to set apart the firstborn of the males for sacred use, either to be sacrificed of clean beasts or redeemed of men and unclean beasts; the ground of the claim, 'it is mine,' that God claimed the firstborn as his own peculiar right, because he had spared Israel's firstborn when he slew the firstborn of Egypt, so that they were his by redemption; the type the commentators draw, of Christ the firstborn, and of believers as 'the church of the firstborn,' sanctified and belonging wholly to God; that what God spares and redeems he claims as his own).

debates.question: Why does God claim all the firstborn as his own?

sides.position: By right of redemption — because he spared Israel's firstborn when he slew the firstborn of Egypt, they are his own peculiar portion; a type of Christ the firstborn and of believers, 'the church of the firstborn' (Hebrews 12:23), sanctified and belonging wholly to God

themes: Sanctify unto me all the firstborn, whatsoever openeth the womb among the children of Israel
both of man and of beast: it is mine
God claiming the firstborn as his own by redemption, having spared them in Egypt
a type of Christ the firstborn and of believers, the church of the firstborn

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Moses said, Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage; the witnesses note Moses bidding the people remember the day they came out of Egypt, the house of bondage, brought out by strength of hand, and eat no leavened bread — the charge to keep the memory of the redemption, and to purge out leaven (Moses pressing the duty of remembrance: 'Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage... there shall no leavened bread be eaten'; the call, 'Remember this day,' that the day of their coming out should be kept in lasting memory, never forgotten; the description of their estate, 'out of the house of bondage,' the cruel slavery from which they were freed; the author of the deliverance, 'by strength of hand the LORD brought you out,' that it was no power of their own but God's mighty hand that wrought it, all the glory his; the ordinance joined to the remembrance, 'there shall no leavened bread be eaten,' that, in memory of their hasty flight, they should eat unleavened bread; the type the commentators draw, of leaven as sin and corruption, to be purged out by the redeemed).

debates.question: What does the unleavened bread typify?

sides.position: Leaven as sin and corruption, to be purged out by the redeemed — kept in memory of the hasty flight from Egypt, and a type for the believer to keep the feast 'with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth' (1 Corinthians 5:7-8)

themes: Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage
there shall no leavened bread be eaten
Moses charging the people to remember the deliverance by God's strength of hand
the type of leaven as sin and corruption, to be purged out by the redeemed

schools.summary: Gill: this day ye came out of Egypt, on the fifteenth of Nisan, as the Targum of Jonathan; the witnesses note Moses marking that on this day, in the month Abib, they came out of Egypt — the fixing of the very time of the deliverance, that it might be exactly kept (the date set down for a lasting memorial: 'This day came ye out in the month Abib'; the precision, 'This day came ye out,' that the exact day was marked, the day of their going out of Egypt, the fifteenth of the month; the month named, 'the month Abib,' the month of green ears, later called Nisan, the first month of the sacred year, made first because of the redemption; the fitness, that the deliverance fell in the spring, the season of new life and harvest beginning, a fit time for the birth of the nation; that God would have the time of his great mercies exactly remembered, the day and the month kept; that the setting of the date roots the feast in real history, no myth but a thing done on a known day).

themes: This day came ye out
in the month Abib
the exact day and month of the deliverance marked for a lasting memorial
the month Abib made first of the sacred year because of the redemption, rooted in real history

schools.summary: Gill: though the whole land was called Canaan, there was one nation in particular so called; the land sworn to the fathers, flowing with milk and honey; the witnesses note God appointing that, when brought into the land sworn to the fathers, flowing with milk and honey, they should keep this service in this month — the feast bound to the inheritance, the ordinance to be kept in the land of promise (God tying the feast to the coming possession: 'when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites... that thou shalt keep this service in this month'; the promise renewed, 'the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites... which he sware unto thy fathers,' that God's bringing them into Canaan, the land sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, was assured, the feast presupposing the certain inheritance; the richness of the land, 'flowing with milk and honey,' the abundant inheritance promised; the charge, 'thou shalt keep this service in this month,' that the feast of unleavened bread should be kept yearly, in this same month, in the land, a perpetual ordinance; that the very command to keep the feast in Canaan was a pledge that they should surely come there).

themes: when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites... a land flowing with milk and honey
that thou shalt keep this service in this month
the feast bound to the inheritance sworn to the fathers
the very command to keep the feast in Canaan a pledge that they should surely come there

schools.summary: Gill: the obligation to eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day a feast to the LORD, a holy convocation; the witnesses note God appointing seven days of eating unleavened bread, with a feast to the LORD on the seventh — the set length of the feast and its solemn close (the ordinance given its measure and its holy day: 'Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread, and in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD'; the term, 'Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread,' that the whole feast should last seven days, through all which only unleavened bread should be eaten; the solemn close, 'in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD,' that the last day should be kept as a feast to the LORD, a holy convocation, a sabbath of rest and sacred assembly to crown the week; that the feast was thus bounded and marked, a stated time of remembrance and worship; the fitness of the number seven, the number of completeness, for a feast of full and finished redemption; that God appoints the times and forms of his worship, that it be done in order).

themes: Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread
and in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD
the seven days of unleavened bread crowned by a holy feast to the LORD
the number seven of completeness fitting a feast of full and finished redemption

schools.summary: Gill: this is very express, that not only were they to abstain from eating leavened bread, but no leaven was to be seen in all their quarters; the witnesses note God charging that unleavened bread be eaten seven days, and no leaven be seen anywhere in all their borders — the thoroughness of the purging of leaven, the whole house and land to be clear of it (God pressing the complete removal of leaven: 'there shall no leavened bread be seen with thee, neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters'; the strictness, 'no leavened bread be seen with thee, neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters,' that not only must they not eat leaven but none must be found or seen anywhere within their borders, the whole land swept clean of it; the thoroughness, that the purging was to be entire, no trace of leaven left, in all their coasts; the type the commentators draw, that as leaven figures sin and corruption, so the redeemed must purge it out wholly, not tolerating the least of it, keeping the feast 'with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth'; that God requires not a partial but a complete putting away of sin).

themes: there shall no leavened bread be seen with thee
neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters
the strict charge that no leaven be seen anywhere in their borders
the type that the redeemed must purge out sin wholly, God requiring a complete putting away

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be shown not to a single child only, but by parents to all their children, sons and daughters, and even unasked, that they may know the reason of eating unleavened bread; the witnesses note the parent charged to show his son that the feast was kept because of what the LORD did when he came out of Egypt — the duty of teaching the children the meaning of the ordinance (the feast becoming the occasion of instruction: 'thou shalt shew thy son in that day, saying, This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt'; the duty laid on parents, 'thou shalt shew thy son,' that the keeping of the feast should be made the occasion of teaching the children, and that not one son only but all, sons and daughters, even unasked; the substance of the teaching, 'This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me,' that the parent should tell the child the reason of the feast, the great deliverance God wrought; the personal owning, 'did unto me,' as though the parent had himself been redeemed, claiming the mercy as his own; the great duty the commentators draw, that the redeemed should diligently teach their children the works of God, that the memory of his mercies be handed down).

themes: thou shalt shew thy son in that day
This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt
the duty of teaching the children the meaning of the feast
the redeemed teaching their children the works of God, that his mercies be handed down

schools.summary: Gill: these are the words of an Israelitish parent to his son, telling him that this feast would serve to refresh his memory with what God did for his people of old; the witnesses note the feast appointed to be a sign on the hand and a memorial between the eyes, that the LORD's law might be in the mouth, since with a strong hand he brought them out — the keeping of the redemption ever in mind and action and speech (the deliverance to govern hand, eye, and tongue: 'it shall be for a sign unto thee upon thine hand, and for a memorial between thine eyes, that the LORD's law may be in thy mouth'; the figures, 'a sign... upon thine hand, and... a memorial between thine eyes,' that the redemption should be kept ever in remembrance, as a thing bound on the hand governing the actions and set before the eyes ever in view, and 'in thy mouth' often spoken of; that the Jews took these words literally for the phylacteries, the frontlets bound on hand and brow, but the commentators rather take the sense as a constant, heart-deep remembrance that should rule all of life; the ground, 'for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt,' that the greatness of the deliverance, wrought by God's strong hand, deserved to be so constantly remembered; that God's great mercies should govern our hands, our eyes, and our tongues).

debates.question: Is 'a sign upon thine hand and a memorial between thine eyes' literal phylacteries?

sides.position: The Jews took it literally, for the phylacteries (frontlets bound on hand and brow)
Rather a figure of a constant, heart-deep remembrance of the redemption that should rule all of life — governing the actions (hand), the sight (eyes), and the speech (mouth)

themes: a sign unto thee upon thine hand, and for a memorial between thine eyes, that the LORD's law may be in thy mouth
for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt
the redemption kept ever in mind, action, and speech
a constant, heart-deep remembrance ruling the hands, the eyes, and the tongue

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: this ordinance the Israelites were to keep at its appointed time, from year to year; Gill notes it is not the ordinance of the phylacteries, as the Targum took it, but the ordinance of unleavened bread; the witnesses note the people charged to keep this ordinance in its season from year to year — the perpetual and stated observance of the feast appointed (God binding the feast as a yearly ordinance: 'Thou shalt therefore keep this ordinance in his season from year to year'; the duty, 'keep this ordinance,' not the ordinance of the phylacteries but the ordinance of unleavened bread, the feast just commanded; the time, 'in his season,' that it should be kept at its appointed time, in the right month and days, not at men's pleasure but as God fixed; the perpetuity, 'from year to year,' that the feast should be a standing, yearly observance, kept by every generation, a lasting memorial of the redemption; that God appoints set and recurring times of remembrance, that his mercies be not forgotten; the order and constancy of God's worship, kept in its season).

themes: Thou shalt therefore keep this ordinance in his season
from year to year
the feast bound as a yearly ordinance at its appointed season
a standing memorial of the redemption for every generation, that his mercies be not forgotten

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: in these verses Moses communicated to the people the law concerning the firstborn, for when the LORD should bring them into the land sworn to their fathers; Gill notes the land of the Canaanites is put for all the rest of the nations; the witnesses note God appointing that, when brought into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware to them and their fathers, and gave it them, the law of the firstborn should be kept — the binding of the law of the firstborn to the coming inheritance (God tying this ordinance also to the possession of the land: 'when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers, and shall give it thee'; the renewed assurance, 'the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites,' that God would surely bring them into Canaan, put for all the nations of the land; the ground, 'as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers,' the covenant oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and to the people, a sure foundation; the gift, 'and shall give it thee,' that the land should be their inheritance by God's free gift, not their own getting; that the law of the firstborn, like the feast, presupposes the certain entering of the land, the command a pledge of the promise; God's faithfulness to his sworn covenant).

themes: when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers
and shall give it thee
the law of the firstborn bound to the coming inheritance
given by God's free gift, the command a pledge of the covenant promise

schools.summary: Gill: 'set apart' explains the word 'sanctify,' showing it signifies separating such to the use and service of God, causing it to pass from a man's own power to be the LORD's; the witnesses note God commanding that all that opened the womb, and every firstling of beast, the males, be set apart to the LORD — the actual consecrating of the firstborn as the LORD's portion (the firstborn to be handed over to God: 'thou shalt set apart unto the LORD all that openeth the matrix... the males shall be the LORD's'; the act, 'set apart unto the LORD,' that the firstborn should be separated from common use and given over wholly to the use and service of God, passing from a man's own power to be the LORD's; the breadth, 'all that openeth the matrix, and every firstling... of a beast,' that both the firstborn of men and the firstlings of beasts were claimed; the limit, 'the males shall be the LORD's,' that it was the firstborn males that were thus God's, in memory of the firstborn males spared in Egypt; that what God claims as his own must be actually given over to him, not kept back; that the redeemed and their increase belong to God, to be devoted to his service).

themes: thou shalt set apart unto the LORD all that openeth the matrix
the males shall be the LORD's
the firstborn of man and beast set apart and given wholly to God
what God claims as his own must be actually given over, the redeemed and their increase belonging to him

schools.summary: Gill: every firstling of an ass was to be redeemed with a lamb, which was given to the priest for it, or else its neck broken; the witnesses note God commanding that the firstling of an ass be redeemed with a lamb, or its neck broken, and all the firstborn of man redeemed — the law of redemption for the unclean and for man, a type of redemption by the Lamb (God appointing a ransom for what could not be offered: 'every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb... and all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem'; the case of the ass, 'every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb,' that the ass, being an unclean beast, could not be offered to God but must be redeemed with a lamb, given to the priest in its stead, or else its neck broken, it being unfit for the LORD's altar; the case of man, 'all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem,' that the firstborn sons, being God's, must yet be redeemed with a price, afterward fixed at five shekels, not sacrificed; the rich type the commentators draw, that the unclean ass redeemed by the lamb figures the sinner, unfit for God, redeemed only by Christ the Lamb who died in his stead; the great gospel truth, that the redeemed are bought with a price, ransomed by the Lamb of God).

debates.question: What does the firstling of the unclean ass redeemed with a lamb typify?

sides.position: The sinner — unclean and unfit for God, who cannot be offered but must be redeemed only by Christ the Lamb, who died in his stead; the redeemed are bought with a price, ransomed by the Lamb of God

themes: every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb
and all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem
the unclean firstling redeemed with a lamb, the firstborn of man with a price
a type of the sinner, unfit for God, redeemed only by Christ the Lamb who died in his stead

schools.summary: Gill: when the son asks in time to come, the day following such a separation or redemption of the firstborn, or at any later time; the witnesses note God appointing that, when the son should ask what this meant, the father should answer that by strength of hand the LORD brought them out of Egypt, the house of bondage — the catechizing of the children by their own questions (the ordinance to be explained to the inquiring child: 'when thy son asketh thee in time to come, saying, What is this? that thou shalt say unto him, By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt'; the occasion, 'when thy son asketh thee... What is this?' that the child, seeing the strange rites of the firstborn, would naturally ask their meaning and so give the parent occasion to teach; the answer appointed, 'By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage,' that the father should point the child to the great deliverance, wrought by God's mighty hand, as the reason of all; the wisdom of God in ordaining rites that should provoke the children's questions and so lead to instruction; the duty of parents to be ready to give a reason of their worship to their children).

themes: when thy son asketh thee in time to come, saying, What is this?
By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage
the child's question made the occasion of teaching the deliverance
the wisdom of God in ordaining rites that provoke the children's questions and lead to instruction

schools.summary: Gill: when Pharaoh showed great reluctance to let them go, with difficulty prevailed upon, the LORD slew all the firstborn of Egypt; the witnesses note the father to tell how, when Pharaoh would hardly let them go, the LORD slew all Egypt's firstborn, and so he sacrifices or redeems the firstborn — the ground of the law of the firstborn in the night of the smiting (the rite traced to the great deliverance: 'when Pharaoh would hardly let us go, that the LORD slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt... therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix... but all the firstborn of my children I redeem'; the occasion recalled, 'when Pharaoh would hardly let us go,' Pharaoh's stubborn refusal, that the LORD slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both of man and beast; the ground of the law, 'therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix... but all the firstborn of my children I redeem,' that, because God spared Israel's firstborn and slew Egypt's, the firstborn of Israel, both clean beasts sacrificed and men and unclean beasts redeemed, were the LORD's; that the law of the firstborn was a standing memorial of that night, owning God's right over the spared; that the redeemed should ever own that they are the LORD's by the right of his sparing mercy).

themes: when Pharaoh would hardly let us go, that the LORD slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt
therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix... but all the firstborn of my children I redeem
the law of the firstborn traced to the night of the smiting and sparing
a standing memorial that the redeemed are God's by the right of his sparing mercy

schools.summary: Gill: these laws concerning the firstlings and the redemption of the firstborn would be a token and memorial, as the feast of unleavened bread before; the witnesses note that these laws of the firstborn should be a token upon the hand and frontlets between the eyes, since by strength of hand the LORD brought them out — the keeping of the redemption ever before them in act and thought (as with the feast, so the firstborn law a constant memorial: 'it shall be for a token upon thine hand, and for frontlets between thine eyes: for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt'; the figure repeated, 'a token upon thine hand, and... frontlets between thine eyes,' that the redemption should be as constantly remembered and as plainly owned as a thing bound on the hand and worn before the eyes, governing the life; that the Jews again took this for the literal phylacteries, but the commentators rather a heart-deep, life-ruling remembrance; the ground once more, 'for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt,' the greatness of the deliverance the reason for so constant a memory; that the great works of God deserve to be kept ever in mind and to shape the whole of life).

themes: it shall be for a token upon thine hand, and for frontlets between thine eyes
for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt
the law of the firstborn, like the feast, a constant token of the redemption
the great works of God kept ever in mind and shaping the whole of life

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here is the choice God made of their way, leading them not the nearest but the safest; the witnesses note God leading Israel not through the near Philistine way, lest they see war and repent and return to Egypt — the wise and tender leading of God, choosing the longer but safer way (God sparing his weak people the trial of war too soon: 'God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near; for God said, Lest... the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt'; the choice, 'God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near,' that the short road by the Philistine country was avoided, though it was the nearest to Canaan; the reason, 'Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt,' that God knew his people, newly freed and faint-hearted, were not yet able to face the warlike Philistines and might, at the sight of war, lose heart and turn back to bondage; the tender wisdom of God, who leads his people not by the nearest but by the way they are able to bear, sparing them trials too great for their faith; that God fits the way to the strength of his people, dealing gently with the weak).

debates.question: Why did God not lead Israel by the near Philistine way?

sides.position: In tender wisdom, lest the newly-freed, faint-hearted people see war with the warlike Philistines, lose heart, and turn back to Egypt; God leads his people not by the nearest but by the way they are able to bear, sparing them trials too great for their faith

themes: God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near
Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt
God's wise and tender leading, the longer but safer way
God leading his people by the way they are able to bear, sparing trials too great for their faith

schools.summary: Gill: instead of going toward Gaza and the Mediterranean, the Lord directed them to turn off toward the wilderness of the Red Sea; the children of Israel went up harnessed, in orderly array; the witnesses note God leading the people about by the way of the wilderness of the Red Sea, and the children of Israel going up harnessed — the roundabout but appointed way, the people marching out in order (God leading them the circuitous way that served his purpose: 'God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea: and the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt'; the way, 'God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea,' that, instead of the short road, God led them by a roundabout way toward the Red Sea, which, though it seemed to entangle them, served God's purpose, both to spare them war and to make a way for his great wonder at the sea; the manner of their going, 'the children of Israel went up harnessed,' or in orderly array, or armed, out of Egypt, that they marched out not as fleeing fugitives in confusion but in order, ranked and equipped, like an army; that God brought them out with dignity and order, his hosts marching forth; that God's roundabout ways for his people are always wise, leading them where his glory and their good require).

themes: God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea
and the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt
the roundabout way serving God's purpose, to spare them war and make way for his wonder at the sea
Israel going up in orderly array like an army, God's roundabout ways always wise

schools.summary: Gill: Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, his remains well called bones since at such a distance from his death the flesh must be gone; the witnesses note Moses taking Joseph's bones with him, for Joseph had strictly sworn Israel that, when God visited them, they should carry up his bones — the keeping of Joseph's dying charge, the faith of the patriarch honoured (the oath made long before now performed: 'Moses took the bones of Joseph with him: for he had straitly sworn the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you; and ye shall carry up my bones'; the care of Moses, that amid all the haste and tumult of the going out, he took the bones of Joseph with him, not forgetting the sacred charge; the oath recalled, that Joseph had straitly sworn the children of Israel, binding them that when God will surely visit you they should carry up his bones to Canaan; the faith of Joseph in that dying charge, that he believed God's promise to bring his seed into the land and would have his bones rest there, a witness to the coming deliverance; that the oath was now honoured, the faith of the patriarch confirmed by the event).

themes: Moses took the bones of Joseph with him: for he had straitly sworn the children of Israel
God will surely visit you; and ye shall carry up my bones away hence with you
Moses keeping Joseph's dying oath amid all the haste of the going out
honouring the faith of the patriarch who believed God's promise (Hebrews 11:22)

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: they encamped in Etham, at the edge of the wilderness, God not leading them the near way; Gill notes it was the second day from their coming out, the sixteenth of Nisan; the witnesses note Israel journeying from Succoth and encamping in Etham, at the edge of the wilderness — the orderly stages of the march under God's leading (the journey proceeding by appointed stages: 'they took their journey from Succoth, and encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness'; the progress, 'they took their journey from Succoth,' on the second day of their going out, removing from their first resting-place; the next station, 'encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness,' at the border of the great wilderness into which God was leading them, the cultivated land now behind, the desert before; that the Scripture records the stages of the march, that the leading of God might be traced step by step; that God brought them by sure stages, encamping where he appointed, the cloud going before; the comfort, that God orders every stage of his people's pilgrimage, none left to chance).

themes: And they took their journey from Succoth
and encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness
the orderly stages of the march under God's leading
God ordering every stage of his people's pilgrimage, none left to chance

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD who went before them is called the Angel of the Lord, not a created but the uncreated Angel, the Angel of Jehovah's presence, the Word and Son of God, the Lord Christ; the witnesses note the LORD going before Israel in a pillar of cloud by day to lead, and a pillar of fire by night to give light, that they might go by day and night — the gracious presence and guidance of God, leading and lighting his people (God himself going before them in the cloud and the fire: 'the LORD went before them by day in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way; and by night in a pillar of fire, to give them light'; who it was that went before, that 'the LORD' here is 'the Angel of the Lord,' not a created angel but the uncreated Angel, the Angel of Jehovah's presence, the Word and Son of God, the Lord Christ, a pre-incarnate presence of Christ leading his people; the double mercy, 'a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way' by day and to shade them from the heat, and 'a pillar of fire, to give them light' by night and to warm and guard them; the constancy, 'to go by day and night,' that God's guidance was unbroken, fitted to every hour; the great comfort, that God himself, in Christ, leads and lights his pilgrim people through the wilderness).

debates.question: Who is the LORD who went before Israel in the pillar of cloud and fire?

sides.position: The Angel of the LORD — not a created angel but the uncreated Angel, the Angel of Jehovah's presence, the Word and Son of God, a pre-incarnate presence of Christ leading and lighting his pilgrim people through the wilderness

themes: the LORD went before them by day in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way
and by night in a pillar of fire, to give them light
the LORD, the uncreated Angel, going before Israel in cloud and fire
a pre-incarnate presence of Christ leading and lighting his pilgrim people

schools.summary: Gill: the pillar always appeared, the cloud by day a guide and shelter, the fire by night, continuing till they came through the wilderness to the borders of the land; the witnesses note that God took not away the pillar of cloud by day nor of fire by night from before the people — the unfailing constancy of God's presence and guidance (God's leading never failing his people: 'He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night, from before the people'; the constancy, 'He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night,' that the cloud and fire were never withdrawn, the cloud always appearing by day as guide and shelter, the fire by night as light and guard; the duration, that this continued all the way till they came through the wilderness to the borders of the land, the whole long journey; the great comfort the commentators draw, that God's presence with his people, once given, was not taken away but abode constantly with them through all their wanderings; the faithfulness of God, who, having undertaken to lead his people, never forsook them, leading them all the way; the type of God's abiding presence with his church, never withdrawn).

themes: He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night
from before the people
the pillar of cloud and fire never taken away, abiding constantly through the whole journey
the faithfulness of God who never forsakes his own, a type of his abiding presence with his church
